suggestions.title: Estimated cost to train a top-tier speech‑to‑text model
suggestions.preview: Training a state‑of‑the‑art speech model (~1 B parameters) on ~10 k hours of labeled audio typically costs $5‑10 M in compute, plus data licensing and engineering overhead, pushing total spend to $10‑20 M.

suggestions.title: Why most teams fine‑tune instead of training from scratch
suggestions.preview: Open‑source pretrained encoders (e.g., Whisper, wav2vec 2.0) let you reach near‑state‑of‑the‑art accuracy with a few thousand GPU hours, cutting cost by >99% versus full‑scale training.

suggestions.title: What cost components should we budget for beyond GPU time?
suggestions.preview: Ask the team to break down expenses: data collection/annotation, storage, engineering labor, and ongoing inference serving, to get a realistic total‑cost‑of‑ownership.

suggestions.title: Rough cost range for top‑tier speech‑to‑text model
suggestions.preview: Training a state‑of‑the‑art speech‑to‑text model from scratch typically costs $50‑$150 M, driven by GPU clusters (≈10k A100 GPUs for 3‑6 months) plus data acquisition, storage, and engineering.

suggestions.title: Open‑source multimodal models are not always trained on the entire internet
suggestions.preview: Most publicly released multimodal LLMs (e.g., LLaVA, Flamingo) are trained on curated subsets (hundreds of billions tokens) rather than the full web; they still miss many domains and require licensing checks.

suggestions.title: What evaluation metric will you use to pick the final transcript?
suggestions.preview: Ask the team which metric (WER, CER, BLEU, or human‑in‑the‑loop scoring) will guide the final LLM judge, so the ensemble’s output aligns with your product’s quality targets.

suggestions.title: Estimated budget for building a SOTA speech‑to‑text system
suggestions.preview: Training a state‑of‑the‑art speech‑to‑text model from scratch typically costs $10‑$100 M, driven by GPU/TPU compute (10‑30 k GPU‑hrs), data licensing, and engineering effort. Fine‑tuning an existing LLM reduces cost to $0.5‑$5 M.

suggestions.title: Clarify training data scope of popular open‑source multimodal models
suggestions.preview: Most open‑source multimodal models are not trained on the entire internet; they use curated subsets such as The Pile, Common Crawl snapshots, and specific audio‑text corpora, covering a few terabytes rather than the full web.

suggestions.title: Use ensemble LLM correction with WER validation
suggestions.preview: Highlight that combining five transcript candidates and a final LLM judge can be measured with word error rate (WER) on a held‑out validation set, ensuring the ensemble actually improves accuracy over any single model.

suggestions.title: Answer: Cost & training approach for SOTA speech‑to‑text
suggestions.preview: Training a brand‑new, large‑scale speech model from scratch typically costs $50‑$100 M in compute alone. Fine‑tuning open‑source LLMs with a curated audio‑text dataset can be done for $0.5‑$2 M using cloud GPUs, plus engineering overhead.

suggestions.title: Fact‑check: LLM exposure to all world languages
suggestions.preview: Large multilingual models have been trained on massive multilingual corpora, but they do not equally cover every language. Low‑resource languages remain under‑represented, so the claim that LLMs have “heard every language” is inaccurate.

suggestions.title: Talk: Data quality & alignment outweigh model size
suggestions.preview: Highlight that high‑quality, well‑aligned audio‑text pairs and a robust error‑correction pipeline often improve word‑error rate more than simply increasing model parameters, especially when fine‑tuning existing multimodal LLMs.

suggestions.title: Answer: Approximate budget & steps for a top speech‑to‑text system
suggestions.preview: Building a world‑class speech‑to‑text model typically costs $30‑$80 M: $15‑$30 M for compute (thousands of GPU‑years), $5‑$15 M for data acquisition/curation, and $10‑$35 M for engineering, licensing, and infrastructure.

suggestions.title: Fact‑check: Do LLMs really see every language on the internet?
suggestions.preview: Most large LLMs have token exposure to >100 languages, but coverage is uneven; low‑resource languages appear in far fewer examples, so performance varies dramatically compared to high‑resource languages.

suggestions.title: Question: Which GPU class and compute hours are realistic for fine‑tuning the ensemble?
suggestions.preview: Ask the team to specify the number of A100‑40GB GPU‑years or TPU‑v4 weeks they plan to allocate for fine‑tuning each of the five base models and the final adjudication model.

chat.content:
[fact check] Fact‑check: Do LLMs really see every language on the internet?

Preview: Most large LLMs have token exposure to >100 languages, but coverage is uneven; low‑resource languages appear in far fewer examples, so performance varies dramatically compared to high‑resource languages.

fromSuggestion.title: Fact‑check: Do LLMs really see every language on the internet?

chat.content:
LLMs do not truly “see every language on the internet”; they are exposed to many languages but coverage is uneven and low‑resource languages are under‑represented.  

- Most state‑of‑the‑art multilingual LLMs (e.g., GPT‑4, LLaMA‑2, PaLM) are trained on web‑scale corpora that include text from > 100 languages, but the amount of data per language varies by orders of magnitude.  
- High‑resource languages (English, Mandarin, Spanish, French, Arabic) each contribute billions of tokens, giving strong performance; low‑resource languages (e.g., many African, Indigenous, or regional languages) may have only thousands‑to‑millions of tokens, leading to much poorer results.  
- Studies (e.g., “Multilingual Language Models: A Survey” 2023) show that performance drops sharply for languages with < 10 M tokens in training data.  
- Consequently, while LLMs have “heard” many languages, they have not seen every language equally, and some languages are effectively absent from their training.  

*Triggered by the claim “LLMs are trained on the whole internet so they have heard every language in the world.”*
